title: Asha Degree: The 9-Year-Old Who Vanished 📁
segments: On February 14, 2000, in Shelby, North Carolina, nine-year-old Asha Degree got out of her bed in the middle of the night, packed a small backpack, and walked alone down Highway 18 in the rain. She was last seen by truck drivers around 3:30 a.m. By morning she was gone.

Asha was the youngest of two children. She lived with her parents Harold and Iquilla Degree and her brother O'Bryant. She was a careful child — described by her teachers as polite, organized, and afraid of the dark. None of that fit what she did that night.

Two truck drivers reported seeing a young girl walking south on Highway 18. One of them, Roy Eskridge, slowed down and tried to ask her what she was doing. According to his account, Asha ran into the woods.

When Asha's parents discovered she was missing the next morning, they called police. The Cleveland County Sheriff's office and the FBI launched an immediate search. Snow had begun to fall. The woods were cold and wet.
A backpack with Asha's belongings was found roughly a mile and a half from her home, near the Turner Lumber Company. It contained schoolwork, a toothbrush, and some of her favorite items.

What she did not pack was telling. She left her glasses behind. Asha had to wear them to see clearly. The fact that she walked away without them suggested she didn't expect to be gone long — or didn't think clearly about it.
Investigators ran down theories from the start. A child running away. A child meeting someone she knew. A child being lured by a stranger. None of them produced a clear lead.
Here's where the case takes a turn. In 2001, a year after Asha vanished, road workers near Shelby found a green Eddie Bauer book bag. Inside were items belonging to Asha — including her clothing.

The bag had been sealed inside a black plastic trash bag and dumped along Highway 18, roughly 30 miles north of where Asha was last seen. The discovery confirmed two things. First, that Asha was almost certainly the victim of a crime. Second, that someone with a vehicle had been involved.

FBI investigators recovered DNA from items inside the bag. They preserved every fiber, every hair, every trace. Genetic analysis was limited by the technology of the time. The samples have been retested multiple times since.

The Cleveland County Sheriff's office made the case a priority for over two decades. Investigators interviewed thousands of locals. They followed up on tips in 36 states. They visited prisons to talk to inmates with knowledge of the area.

In 2016, the FBI publicly released information that Asha had been seen carrying a particular item not normally associated with her — a green book bag with white piping. They asked anyone who had seen the bag to come forward.
Tips poured in. None broke the case open.
In 2018, the Cleveland County Sheriff's Department reported they had developed new persons of interest based on DNA results. They did not name the individuals. They asked the public for patience.

By 2024, investigators had used genetic genealogy to identify two suspects. In December 2024, the FBI executed search warrants at properties in North Carolina connected to a married couple. The names that emerged in court records were Roy Lee Dedmon and Connie Dedmon.

The Dedmons had lived near Shelby in 2000. Their granddaughter Hannah Mecca-Dedmon had given birth to a stillborn child four days before Asha disappeared. The pregnancy and the timing have become central to investigators' working theory.

Federal investigators have not publicly stated what they believe happened. They have confirmed only that the search warrants were executed in connection with Asha Degree. The Dedmons have not been formally charged with her abduction or death.
What investigators have found, according to court filings, includes communication records, fiber evidence, and financial records relevant to the timeline.

Asha's parents Harold and Iquilla have continued to advocate publicly for the case. Iquilla has spoken in interviews about every February 14 since 2000. The family runs the Asha Degree Foundation, which supports missing-children causes.

The Asha Degree case is one of the longest active FBI child-abduction investigations in modern history. Her photo has been distributed on milk cartons, billboards, and digital missing-children networks worldwide.
Forensic teams continue to test items from the 2001 backpack discovery. Each round of testing produces more information. The sheriff's office has committed to seeing the case through.

If you remember anything about the Shelby area on February 14, 2000, or about activity along Highway 18 between Shelby and Morganton in the days after, the FBI's Charlotte field office maintains a tip line dedicated to the Asha Degree case.
A reward of 50,000 dollars remains active. The phone number is published on the FBI's missing persons page.
Asha would be 35 years old in 2025. Her family still leaves a chair for her on her birthday and at Christmas dinner. Their hope is documented. So is their patience.
